macro_events.event_summary: The ECB selected open European standards for the digital euro infrastructure, explicitly sidelining Visa and Mastercard from the rails.
primary_drivers: Loss of European interchange/processing revenue for US card networks
Sovereign payment rail disintermediation reducing network effects
Regulatory precedent risk for card networks in other jurisdictions

beneficiaries.rationale: ACI Worldwide provides real-time payment infrastructure software and has positioned for European instant-payment/CBDC rails; an open-standards digital euro favors infrastructure vendors over closed networks.
Core processors with European bank relationships could be tapped to integrate digital euro into existing bank rails, bypassing card networks.

losers.rationale: Visa derives ~25% of net revenue from Europe; an ECB-mandated open-standard digital euro directly disintermediates Visa's rails for euro-denominated retail payments and sets a precedent other central banks may follow.

Mastercard has even higher European revenue mix (~30%) than Visa and is explicitly named as sidelined; loss of euro-area volume on a future government-backed rail compresses long-term TAM and pricing power.

PayPal's European checkout business competes with bank-rail alternatives; a free, government-backed digital euro at point-of-sale erodes PayPal's value proposition for euro transactions where Europe is a major revenue region.

American Express has smaller but premium European card volume; precedent of central-bank-backed rails sidelining closed networks is a structural negative for the closed-loop model, though AmEx's affluent niche provides some insulation.

headlines: ECB Picks Open European Standards for Digital Euro, Sidelining Visa and Mastercard
